amber.description: Catalysts, regulatory proteins, molecular machines, receptors

black.description: Terminal products, cellular outcomes, equilibrium states, system endpoints

darkSkyBlue.description: Biochemical reactions, transport, modifications, transcription, translation

green.description: External signals, nutrient availability, environmental conditions, initial states

lightCyan.description: Metabolites, molecular complexes, cellular states, compounds

purple.description: Multi-component requirements, signal integration, convergence points

yellow.description: Decision points with alternative pathways (yes/no branching)

description: Nitrogen catabolite repression in S. cerevisiae allows cells to preferentially use high-quality nitrogen sources (ammonia, glutamine) and repress genes for poor nitrogen utilization until needed. Under good nitrogen, TOR kinase activates, Gln3/Gat1 transcription factors are phosphorylated and cytoplasmic. Under nitrogen starvation, TOR inactivates, Sit4 phosphatase dephosphorylates Gln3/Gat1, they enter nucleus and activate NCR genes for alternative nitrogen sources (proline, urea, amino acids). The pathway integrates with GAAC (Gcn4) amino acid starvation response.

name: Nitrogen Catabolite Repression (NCR) and TORC1 Signaling
notes: Nitrogen metabolism shows high OR gates (9) for alternative nitrogen sources. Integrates with TOR signaling.
scientificAccuracy: Based on extensive research on yeast nitrogen metabolism. All components validated.

sources.title: GLN3-mediated transcriptional regulation of the nitrogen catabolite repression-sensitive gene DAL5 in Saccharomyces cerevisiae requires TOR2
The nitrogen catabolite repression-sensitive transcription factor Gln3 is regulated by TOR kinase and the type 2A phosphatase Sit4 in Saccharomyces cerevisiae
Nitrogen Homeostasis in Yeast: An Integrated View
sources.authors: Ljungdahl, PO.; Daignan-Fornier, B.; Gaxiola, RA.
